turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson is a little under the weather today and will participate in this morning's oral argument remotely and it just so happens that she has one of the Court's announcements this morning in case 21-1270, MOAC Mall Holdings v. Transform Holdco. Justice Jackson.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: The Bankruptcy Code sometimes permits the bankrupt debtor to sell or lease the bankruptcy estates property. An interested party that objects to such a transaction can appeal if the Bankruptcy Court authorizes the sale or lease over its objection. This case is about a code provision that limits the permissible effects of such an appeal if it is successful. Summarize, Section 363(m) of Title 11 of the United States Code provides that a successful appeal of a judicial authorization to sell or lease estate property does not invalidate the sale or lease if it was made to a good faith purchaser or lessee unless the authorization and the sale or lease were stayed pending appeal. We took this case to decide whether Section 363(m) limits are either jurisdictional, which is a label that entails several unavoidable consequences, such as the Court being able to raise its requirements at anytime sua sponte or non-jurisdictional and thus potentially excusable under doctrines like waiver or forfeiture. That question arises here in the context of a dispute over a lease at the Minnesota Mall of America. As the code permits, bankrupt debtor, Sears, Roebuck and Co. sought to assign a lease, which was originally between Sears and petitioner MOAC Mall Holdings to the respondents, a company called Transform Holdco. The Bankruptcy Court authorized the assignment of the lease to Transform over MOAC's objection, and MOAC sought to appeal. During the course of the litigation, Transform pledged to the Bankruptcy Court that it would not try to invoke Section 363(m) against MOAC's appeal, but Transform backtracked on that pledge after MOAC won the appeal. And because the lower courts thought Section 363(m) was jurisdictional they were unable to apply the doctrines of judicial estoppel or waiver to bar this apparent gamesmanship. The lower courts ultimately accepted Transform's Section 363(m) arguments and dismissed MOAC's appeal for lack of jurisdiction. We disagree with that judgment. For the reasons given in the written opinion that we filed with the Court today, we first reject Transform's claim that because the Mall of America's leasehold has already left the bankruptcy estate and there is no legally available path to get it back MOAC's claims are moot. We routinely reject these kinds of mootness arguments because it is not for us to act as a Court of First view to confirm contestable merits claims about what might happen on remand. As for the actual question presented, we only treat preconditions to relief like Section 363(m) as jurisdictional if Congress clearly commands it. Congress ordinarily enacts preconditions to relief to facilitate the fair and orderly disposition of litigation. So we will not lightly assume that Congress intends for preconditions to have a jurisdictional character that threatens to up in that orderly progress. The text and context of Section 363(m) lack a clear statement that the provision is jurisdictional. Far from presenting as a limit on judicial power, Section 363(m) takes as a given the exercise of appellate judicial authority over certain authorizations and simply add the heavily caveated proviso that sometimes the Appellate Court's exercise of power may not accomplish all that an appellate wishes. Transform's problem is that Congress can issue important but not jurisdictional commands. And it is hardly clear to us that Section 363(m) is anything more than that. Because Section 363(m) is not jurisdictional, we vacate the Second Circuit's judgment which rested on the contrary view. We remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The Court's opinion is unanimous.
